tweets.text:
In late June, fears surrounding the Mag-7 peaked on the view that AI spending couldn’t be justified & would never generate an adequate ROI (Spenders vs. Receivers) & ironically, that marked a local low.

Fast forward 3-weeks, and after the capex trade became crowded, the narrative has flipped. Now, because China’s Kimi is approaching frontier-model performance at a lower cost, many are assuming hyperscalers no longer need to keep investing at the same pace.

The rebuttal seems quite simple: if AI gets cheaper, usage should explode, not slow.

More enterprises adopting AI, more agents running autonomously, & more applications reaching production all mean one thing: more inference, which ultimately requires more infrastructure.

Any hyperscaler that cuts AI capex too aggressively risks being capacity constrained just as demand accelerates, whilst competitors that continue investing stand to capture incremental market share.

If anything, cheaper intelligence should expand the total AI market, making this a net positive for long-term infrastructure spending rather than a reason to cut it.
tweets.full_text:
In late June, fears surrounding the Mag-7 peaked on the view that AI spending couldn’t be justified & would never generate an adequate ROI (Spenders vs. Receivers) & ironically, that marked a local low.

Fast forward 3-weeks, and after the capex trade became crowded, the narrative has flipped. Now, because China’s Kimi is approaching frontier-model performance at a lower cost, many are assuming hyperscalers no longer need to keep investing at the same pace.

The rebuttal seems quite simple: if AI gets cheaper, usage should explode, not slow.

More enterprises adopting AI, more agents running autonomously, & more applications reaching production all mean one thing: more inference, which ultimately requires more infrastructure.

Any hyperscaler that cuts AI capex too aggressively risks being capacity constrained just as demand accelerates, whilst competitors that continue investing stand to capture incremental market share.

If anything, cheaper intelligence should expand the total AI market, making this a net positive for long-term infrastructure spending rather than a reason to cut it.
